findings.summary: Two independent Model base classes with overlapping responsibility exist in src/core/model.py and src/models/base.py, fragmenting the inheritance hierarchy
evidence: src/core/model.py defines class Model with full case machinery, precision weights, connections, update dispatch, and case history

src/models/base.py defines a separate class Model (also named Model) that re-imports Case from src.core.model but reimplements __init__, case property/setter, and data_buffer — a structural duplication of the same abstraction
src/models/linear_regression.py inherits from src/models/base.py Model, not from src/core/model.py Model, so it lacks precision weights, connections, and _case_history present in the core version
src/models/__init__.py exports 'Model' and 'Case' from .base, shadowing the canonical core exports when both are imported
src/models/base.py uses bare logging.info() (root logger) instead of a named logger, unlike src/core/model.py which uses logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
findings.suggestion: Eliminate src/models/base.py by having LinearRegressionModel inherit directly from src.core.model.Model; remove the duplicate Model class and re-export from core.

findings.summary: Many intra-package modules use absolute 'from src.X' imports instead of relative imports, coupling them to a specific install/sys.path layout
evidence: src/models/base.py: 'from src.core.model import Case' (absolute) while sibling src/models/linear_regression.py uses 'from .base import Model, Case' (relative)
src/cases/__init__.py: 'from src.core.model import Case' (absolute) while every case module in the same package uses relative '..core.model'
src/models/insect/base.py: 'from src.core.active_inference import ActiveInferenceModel' and 'from src.core.model import Case'
src/core/active_inference.py and src/core/neural_network.py: 'from src.core.model import Model, Case' — sibling files that should use relative imports
Over 30 files in src/ mix absolute src.* imports with relative imports; the pattern is not consistently one or the other
findings.suggestion: Standardise on relative imports throughout the src package. Replace all 'from src.X' with 'from .X' or 'from ..X' as appropriate to the module's position.

findings.summary: CaseManager.transform_case() has an else-branch that falls back to model.case = target_case directly, but case_handlers is always a complete dict covering all 8 cases, making the else unreachable and the boundary undefined
evidence: case_handlers is initialised with all 8 Case enum members (NOMINATIVE through VOCATIVE) at __init__ time
transform_case() checks 'if target_case in self.case_handlers' then falls back to 'model.case = target_case' in the else — but with a complete mapping this path is never reachable
Same pattern repeated in process_update() and calculate_free_energy() — each has an unreachable else branch that bypasses the handler layer entirely
findings.suggestion: Remove the unreachable else branches, or convert the conditional to an assertion. If extensibility for future cases is intended, document the contract explicitly.

findings.summary: src/models/insect/cases/ introduces six new domain-specific case types (PheromonalCase, SwarmCase, etc.) that are not members of the core Case enum, creating a parallel case system with no shared protocol
evidence: src/models/insect/__init__.py exports PheromonalCase, SwarmCase, MetamorphicCase, CasteCase, SubstrateCase, StigmergicCase
These are not values in the Case enum (src/core/model.py) and cannot be registered with CaseManager.case_handlers
CaseManager only knows about the 8 canonical Case enum values; insect-specific cases are entirely invisible to the shared coordination layer
This means transform_case(), process_update(), and calculate_free_energy() in CaseManager silently do not apply to any insect-domain case
findings.suggestion: Either extend the Case enum with insect-specific members, or define a shared CaseProtocol/ABC that CaseManager can discover and dispatch dynamically, so domain extensions participate in the shared infrastructure.

findings.summary: Sibling case modules follow inconsistent class-level conventions: 5 of 8 define PRECISION and/or VALID_TRANSITIONS class constants, 3 (NominativeCase, AccusativeCase, DativeCase) do not
evidence: AblativeCase: PRECISION = 1.1, VALID_TRANSITIONS = [Case.NOMINATIVE]
LocativeCase: PRECISION = 0.9, VALID_TRANSITIONS = [Case.ABLATIVE]
InstrumentalCase: PRECISION = 0.8 (no VALID_TRANSITIONS)
GenitiveCase: PRECISION = 1.0 (no VALID_TRANSITIONS)
VocativeCase: PRECISION = 2.0 (no VALID_TRANSITIONS)
NominativeCase, AccusativeCase, DativeCase: neither PRECISION nor VALID_TRANSITIONS defined
AblativeCase.VALID_TRANSITIONS restricts to only [Case.NOMINATIVE], while InsectModel._validate_case_transformation allows 6-7 transitions from ABLATIVE — the two sources of truth disagree
findings.suggestion: Define PRECISION and VALID_TRANSITIONS on every case class (or on none). Reconcile VALID_TRANSITIONS values between the case-module constants and InsectModel's transition validation dict.

findings.summary: Sibling case modules expose inconsistent extended API surfaces: only some case classes implement methods beyond the shared apply/process_update/calculate_free_energy/get_parameters protocol
evidence: AccusativeCase: adds accept_updates(model, updates, validation_func)
AblativeCase: adds emit_to(source, target, payload) and can_transition_to(target_case)
VocativeCase: adds invoke(model, invocation), broadcast(models, message), get_invocation_history(model, limit)
DativeCase: adds receive_data(model, data, data_type)
NominativeCase and InstrumentalCase: no extra methods beyond the shared four
CaseManager uses only apply, process_update, and calculate_free_energy — the unique extended methods are effectively dead API with no callers inside the package
findings.suggestion: Either define a shared protocol (ABC or Protocol class) that all case handlers must implement, or move the extended-method logic into the model's own _update_X methods where the case-specific behavior already lives.

findings.summary: Logger naming convention is inconsistent across sibling modules: most use getLogger(__name__) but several use hardcoded string names
evidence: src/models/linear_regression.py: logging.getLogger("cerebrum-linear-regression")
src/core/model_registry.py: logging.getLogger("cerebrum-model-registry")
src/utils/animation.py: logging.getLogger("cerebrum-animation")
src/utils/fix_animations.py: logging.getLogger("cerebrum-animation-fix")
src/models/base.py: bare logging.info() (root logger), no logger variable at all
All case modules (nominative.py through vocative.py) and core/model.py correctly use getLogger(__name__)
findings.suggestion: Standardise all modules to 'logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)'. Replace hardcoded names and bare logging.X() calls. The hardcoded names break the hierarchy-based filtering that __name__ provides.

findings.summary: src/models/ contains its own base.py with a Model class and case_definitions.py, creating a shadow case sub-system alongside src/cases/ and src/core/
evidence: src/models/__init__.py exports Model and Case from .base, and CaseDefinitions from .case_definitions — separate from the canonical src/core exports
src/models/base.py duplicates the Model class (different implementation, same name)
src/utils/visualization.py imports 'from src.models.base import Case' and 'from src.models.case_definitions import CaseDefinitions', binding it to the shadow system
The package name 'models' implies it should contain model implementations, not alternative base class definitions that compete with src/core/
findings.suggestion: Move case_definitions.py into src/cases/ or src/core/. Remove src/models/base.py and redirect src/models/__init__.py to re-export from src.core. The models package should contain only concrete model implementations.

findings.summary: src/lexicon/__init__.py declares __license__ = 'MIT' while pyproject.toml declares the project license as CC BY-NC-ND 4.0
evidence: src/lexicon/__init__.py line 15: __license__ = "MIT"
pyproject.toml line 6: license = {text = "CC BY-NC-ND 4.0"}
findings.suggestion: Remove the __license__ attribute from src/lexicon/__init__.py or align it with the project-level CC BY-NC-ND 4.0 declaration in pyproject.toml.

findings.summary: src/models/insect/__init__.py declares __version__ and __author__ independently from pyproject.toml, creating a maintenance split for version metadata
evidence: src/models/insect/__init__.py lines 70-71: __version__ = "0.1.0", __author__ = "CEREBRUM Development Team"
pyproject.toml: version = "1.5.0", author = "Daniel Ari Friedman"
The sub-package version (0.1.0) is already out of sync with the project version (1.5.0); the author strings differ
findings.suggestion: Remove __version__ and __author__ from the sub-package __init__.py; reference importlib.metadata if version is needed at runtime.
